era_context: Late Old Kingdom Egypt (Dynasty VI); Early Dynastic III Mesopotamia; Indus Valley Civilisation in its mature phase. Writing systems are well-established in river valleys. The world is predominantly preliterate outside of Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Indus Valley.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and the Steppe (sparse archaeological evidence)
East Asia beyond the Yellow River (limited documentation)

events.title: Old Kingdom Egypt — Dynasty VI reign period
events.description: The year -3092 falls within the reign of a Dynasty VI pharaoh. The Old Kingdom is in its later phases, with evidence suggesting gradual weakening of centralised authority, though the pharaonic system remains intact. Inscriptions from tombs and temples attest to continued administrative activity, military campaigns, and trade. The period is marked by the development of more elaborate tomb inscriptions (Pyramid Texts) and the consolidation of a professional bureaucracy.
key_figures: Dynasty VI pharaoh (identity uncertain without chronological specification)
events.certainty_note: The year -3092 is assigned to the Old Kingdom Dynasty VI period based on conventional chronology, but the exact dating is uncertain by ±100+ years.
cross_references: Part of Old Kingdom Dynasty VI

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for the year -3092. The assignment of this year to Dynasty VI is based on conventional chronology, which is disputed. Some scholars argue for significantly different dating.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -3092 is extremely sparse. No specific events are documented for this year. The period is known only through king lists and general archaeological evidence. Chronological uncertainty is substantial.
